title: Advanced CPU Performance: Speculative Execution, Predication, Out-of-order, Register Renaming, Hyperthreading
subtopics: Register renaming for WAR and WAW

definitions.description: Executing all possible branch outcomes in parallel before knowing which is correct; results of the wrong path are discarded.
Each instruction carries a predicate (Boolean condition); the instruction has effect only if its predicate is true. Eliminates branch instructions at the cost of executing both paths.
Instructions begin or complete execution in a different order than written, when no data dependency prevents it, to fill pipeline stalls.
True dependency: instruction 2 reads a value written by instruction 1. Cannot be eliminated by renaming.
Anti-dependency: instruction 2 writes to a resource that instruction 1 reads. Can be eliminated by register renaming.
Output dependency: both instructions write to the same resource. Can be eliminated by register renaming.
Mapping architectural registers to a larger set of physical registers to eliminate WAR and WAW dependencies.
A single physical processor presents itself as two virtual processors to the OS by duplicating state components (registers) while sharing execution resources.

examples.name: Predication example
examples.description: if(R1==0){R2=5; R3=8;} else R2=21; – implemented with predicate registers P1/P2 instead of jump instructions.

examples.name: Out-of-order example
examples.description: 'in al,278; add bl,al; mov edx,[ebp+8]' – third instruction has no dependency, can complete before the first two.

examples.name: Selection adder with speculative execution
examples.description: Compute sum for both carry-in=0 and carry-in=1 simultaneously; select correct result when carry is known.

learning_objectives: Explain speculative execution and its trade-offs
Describe predication and how it eliminates control dependencies
Explain out-of-order execution and when it is applicable
Identify RAW, WAR, and WAW data dependencies
Explain how register renaming resolves WAR and WAW dependencies
Describe hyperthreading and its benefits and limitations
